case_name: Virginia Dodson-Stephens v. Metropolitan Government of Nashville and Davidson County

summary.brief: Virginia Dodson-Stephens v. Metropolitan Government of Nashville and Davidson County. Medical malpractice case involving obstetrics gynaecology.

evidence_index.text: The child at issue, Courtlynn, was born in 2001. In 2003, Courtlynn’s biological mother was hospitalized for domestic abuse and left two-year-old Courtlynn in the physical care of a teenage co-worker.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Mother later learned that Courtlynn’s biological mother was addicted to drugs and alcohol, and Courtlynn had been born premature and hospitalized for three weeks due to neonatal abstinence syndrome.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: She was diagnosed with post-traumatic stress disorder and reactive attachment disorder and placed on medication.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The trial court found that both defendants were negligent and that each was fifty percent at fault for the child’s injuries.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The trial court found that the plaintiffs should be awarded total compensatory damages of $10,902,348.02, but it reduced the judgment to $600,000 ($300,000 against each defendant) in accordance with the applicable statutory caps.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Mother testified regarding Courtlynn’s self-harm over the years and Courtlynn’s efforts to harm or kill Mother.
evidence_index.speaker: Mother's testimony

evidence_index.text: The trial judge found that Mr. Ballentine 'lied in his statement at the time of the incident,' lied approximately eight times during his deposition, and denied leaving his classroom unsupervised for ten minutes at trial under oath despite video evidence confirming that he did.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The evidence does not preponderate against the trial court’s finding of foreseeability as an element of proximate cause.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The trial court’s findings regarding foreseeability are, therefore, entitled to a presumption of correctness.
evidence_index.speaker: Court reasoning

evidence_index.text: For the aforementioned reasons, we affirm the decision of the circuit court.
evidence_index.speaker: Court conclusion

patient_demographics.relevant_social_history: History of severe mental health issues and institutional care

initial_state.chief_complaint: Paralyzing injuries sustained from jumping out of a school window
initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Courtlynn, a child with a history of severe mental health disorders, was left unsupervised in a classroom and jumped out of a window after reportedly hearing voices.
past_medical_history: Major depressive disorder with psychotic features
initial_state.initial_working_problem: Severe mental health issues with risk of self-harm

requestables.name: Behavioral Intervention Assessment
requestables.description: Assessment to address behavioral needs and concerns

reveal.clinical_significance: Would have identified need for safety plan
reveal.decision_impact: Could have prompted implementation of safety measures

requestables.name: IEP Meeting
requestables.description: Meeting to establish Individualized Education Program

reveal.clinical_significance: Would have addressed educational and safety needs
reveal.decision_impact: Could have ensured appropriate supervision and support

key_events.event: Enrollment without behavioral assessment or IEP meeting

timeline_phases.name: Incident Leading to Injury
timeline_phases.description: Courtlynn jumped out of a second-story window

key_events.event: Courtlynn left unsupervised, jumped out window

decision_points.clinical_context: Courtlynn enrolled in Transition Program without behavioral assessment
decision_points.prompt: Should a behavioral intervention assessment be conducted?

options.clinical_reasoning: Necessary to identify and address behavioral risks

actual_action_defendant.description: Assessment was delayed and not conducted before injury

expected_action_court.description: Assessment should have been conducted immediately

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to conduct assessment increased risk of harm
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Assessment would have identified need for safety measures

decision_points.clinical_context: Courtlynn enrolled without an IEP meeting
decision_points.prompt: Should an IEP meeting be scheduled immediately?

options.description: Schedule IEP meeting within 10 days of enrollment
options.clinical_reasoning: Critical for addressing educational and safety needs

actual_action_defendant.description: IEP meeting was delayed and not conducted before injury

expected_action_court.description: IEP meeting should have been scheduled immediately

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Delay in IEP meeting left educational and safety needs unmet
explanation.what_should_have_happened: IEP meeting would have ensured appropriate support

patient_outcome.description: Courtlynn is paralyzed from the waist down, with loss of bladder and bowel control

key_findings: Failure to conduct behavioral assessment and IEP meeting
Negligence by both Metro and DCS contributed to injury

malpractice_determination.point_of_failure: Failure to conduct necessary assessments and meetings
malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Proper assessments and meetings could have prevented injury

factual_timeline.event: Courtlynn jumped out of a second-story window

allegations.allegation: Failure to conduct behavioral assessment and IEP meeting
